data.title: German Labor Market Improves In November
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

German unemployment dropped by 25,700, bringing the unemployment figure to 2.885 million, a surprisingly positive outcome. According to the just-released data, seasonally adjusted unemployment increased by 1,000, keeping the unemployment rate unchanged at 6.3%.

Today’s labour market numbers will bring some relief, at least in the political debate. The feared worsening of the labour market that emerged after the number of unemployed reached the symbolic three-million mark in August has so far been avoided. That said, since reaching a low of 2.2 million in May 2022, the number of unemployed has steadily increased. It's not up by some half a million compared with then.

This trajectory reflects textbook economics: with the economy effectively stagnating for over five years and industry facing severe structural challenges, a worsening of the labour market was just a matter of time.

Improvements, yes, but far from a turning point

Looking ahead, recruitment plans in both manufacturing and services have continued to weaken, and the number of vacancies is down to levels last seen during the pandemic. Still, other indicators like social media vacancies and hiring indicators at least point to some bottoming-out. At the same time, ongoing announcements of potential cost-cutting measures across the automotive and other industries, along with the continuing increase in some bankruptcies, suggest that things could get worse before they get better.

With the worsening labour market, political uncertainty about the future of Germany’s pension system, and a broader sense of sombreness in the economy, it is no surprise that private consumption has worsened again. After a brief indulgence at the turn of last year, German consumers have again closed their wallets. This morning’s news that retail sales dropped by 0.3% month-on-month in October just strengthens this point. Even more so, as real wages were still up by almost 3% on the year in the third quarter, and the savings rate has almost come down to pre-pandemic levels - a statistical conundrum.

Overall, despite today’s favourable news from the labour market, a turning point is clearly not in sight. Instead, the very gradual worsening of the German labour market is likely to continue, clearly complicating any comeback of private consumption.

data.title: Fast Growing Circle Is A Bargain At Near IPO Valuation
data.description:
Circle Internet Group, Inc. (CRCL), after a post-IPO meteoric rise of more than 280%, has now fallen back to Earth, trading near its IPO price of $69/share at the time of this writing.

This puts the stock squarely in "value" territory despite the rapid growth expected from its underlying business.

USDC Reserve Income Resistant to Rate Cuts

The lion's share of Circle's income comes from one source - interest on the US dollar reserve that backs the issuance of USDC stablecoin. And as I pointed out in my previous article about Circle, this line item is resistant to rate cuts from the Fed, as any interest rate reduction is more than offset by the rapid growth of USDC.

Let's test this claim a little further with the latest data.

Per their Q3 earnings release, USDC in circulation was $73.7B, but it has since grown further to $75.2B based on real-time data as of writing. I'll use this as an approximation for the 2025 year-end number.

USDC has been growing at a 3-digit clip, coming in at 108% as of Q3. Despite this, management conservatively guided to 40% CAGR, while noting on the conference call that the industry expects a growth rate of 60% from USDC.

In this analysis, I will consider 3 scenarios: Base Case of 40% growth from management's conservative guidance, Expected Case of 60% from industry research, and Best Case of maintaining 100% growth, which is a possible outcome given the recent regulatory clarity from the GENIUS Act leading to accelerated adoption of USDC (the world's largest regulated stablecoin) by the mainstream economy.

In terms of distribution margin, Q3 came in at 39%, and management guided to 38%. This margin is Circle's income net of distribution costs, and it is expected to expand over the long run, because as USDC's market position strengthens and they diversify its base of distributors, Circle's negotiation power should increase.

That said, I will conservatively apply the 38% number from management.

For the Reserve Return Rate, I will use the US Treasury yield curve for estimation:

Putting it all together, here's what I expect over the next 3 years:

As you can see, not only is reserve income resistant to rate cuts throughout the cycle, it is expected to expand 2x to 7x during the period after distribution costs.

At Circle's current market cap of $17B, it is trading at 6x 2028 net reserve income under the base case scenario, and 2x under the best case scenario - putting it in deep value territory.

Gangbuster Growth from "Other Revenue"

Circle has multiple other income sources aside from USDC reserves.

They are the issuer of EURC - already the world's largest EUR-backed stablecoin despite its nascent market value of $325M. And they also issue yield products such as the USDY.

They are the operator of the ARC blockchain, putting them in competition with Ethereum and Solana but with the advantage of being stablecoin-native. CEO Jeremy Allaire has some very ambitious goals for it, calling ARC the "Economic OS of the Internet".

Perhaps most importantly, USDC is getting a lot of traction in integrating with the real economy and getting accepted as payment - a trend that is accelerating since the GENIUS Act, which I covered in more detail in my previous article.

From a very low base, "Other Revenue" as a whole grew a mind-boggling 50,000% in Q3 year-over-year to $28.5M. For the full year, management raised its guidance for other revenue to $90-100M, which implies an astronomical growth rate that may not be sustainable in the long run.

At this early stage of hypergrowth, it's very difficult to accurately model what this line item will look like in a few years. However, even as the growth rate comes back down to a more pedestrian triple-digit (which is very achievable given the traction they're getting from various initiatives), one can see that the other revenue line is expected to become very material to Circle's business very quickly - most certainly within 2-3 years' time.

Conclusion

Circle's stock price has recently dropped precipitously in sympathy with a correction in the larger crypto market, and perhaps exacerbated by an expected insider lockup expiration.

However, neither event has any material impact on Circle's underlying business in the long, medium, or even short term.

USDC's circulation will outgrow any expected effects from the Fed's rate cuts. Reserve income from USDC alone is enough to justify Circle's current valuation.

In addition, investors brave enough to build a position in Circle during this period of market pessimism are rewarded with a free option on Circle becoming a full-fledged financial institution serving multiple facets of the digital economy.

data.title: Bank OZK: Held Back By Familiar Concerns
data.description:
Credit concerns still appear to be weighing on Arkansas-based lender Bank OZK (OZK). Its shares had been tracking the wider regional bank space in the initial months following my last update, only to fall away in the past few weeks after several banks disclosed losses on loans made to financial borrowers. While OZK wasn't one of them, it did release lighter-than-expected third quarter results around the same time, with slightly mixed developments on the credit front to boot. As a result, OZK has been a frustrating performer since my last piece back in April, trailing the industry and the S&P 500 by double-digit margins.

OZK has delivered this performance despite being one of the cheaper U.S. banks I've covered, trading for around its tangible book value despite historically earning a strong double-digit return on tangible equity.

OZK's followers know what deal is here: this bank has enjoyed huge success over the years in making high-yield construction loans. While notionally a risky category of lending, OZK has simultaneously enjoyed an unusually low rate of credit losses. Indeed, notwithstanding the composition of its loan book, this has allowed OZK to raise its dividend every year since its IPO in the late 1990s. While that is no mean feat for a bank stock, OZK's business model requires it to go out and earn its underwriting spurs in every cycle.

Earnings A Little Muted, But That Was Expected

OZK earned $1.59 per share in net income last quarter. Although a dime lighter than analysts expected, the outlook for its bottom line has actually improved a nudge this year, with consensus now pointing to $6.20 in 2025 EPS, a little better compared to my last piece. Still, this is virtually the same as the $6.14 per share OZK earned in 2024.

To be clear, this relatively muted performance was always expected. It's really just a function of OZK's asset base, as the bank's loan book skews over 80% to variable-rate loans, around 80% of which are indexed off 1-month SOFR. In contrast, many other banks have seen a nice tailwind coming through from the repricing of pre-2022 fixed-rate cashflows.

OZK does have levers it can pull to offset this (rate floors on its variable-rate loans, lowering its relatively expensive deposit costs, and so on), but the reality is that its assets reprice quicker than its liabilities, and this has led to net interest margin contraction. Indeed, despite period-average balances of interest-earning assets growing 10% to $36.9 billion, OZK's net interest income grew just 3% over the first three quarters of the year, with its NIM down 30 bps to 4.34%.

One feature of NIM-driven revenue growth is that there isn't really any incremental expense growth associated with it. Of course, this works in reverse, too, and this has been a headwind for OZK this year. The bank is still taking on new employees and opening up new branches to service its volume-related growth, but without levers to pull to offset lower NIM, costs rose 11% over the first three quarters to $459 million. As a result of these trends, OZK saw over 800bps of negative operating leverage over the first nine months, driving pre-provision net revenue down 1% to $828 million.

Familiar Concerns

The above isn't really a big deal. Yes, it's a worse performance than many banks this year, but as I said, it's really a function of OZK's balance sheet and where we are in the interest rate cycle.

On top of this, two other concerns may be holding the stock back. The obvious one is credit quality. Specifically, over a quarter of OZK's $33 billion loan book is designated as construction and land development. Housed in its Real Estate Specialties Group unit, this has been a very profitable endeavor for the bank historically, as not only do these loans have high yields to begin with, but OZK has also suffered very few losses on them. That combination has given OZK very high risk-adjusted loan margins, ultimately fueling a very strong ~1.9% average return on assets over the past decade.

Of course, macro developments have asked serious questions of this business model in recent times, as construction projects green-lit a few years ago may have seen their economics shift significantly due to higher interest rates.

Loan concentration has also been an issue for OZK. While other banks would adopt a much more granular (and less risky) approach to their loan books, OZK hasn't shied away from writing relatively big individual credits. This came to the fore last year, when concerns about leasing activity related to a particularly chunky loan sparked a large sell-off in the shares.

More recently, OZK reported an uptick in net losses in its most recent quarter. While a 0.41% net charge-off rate is hardly poor – it would be good for most banks and still leaves OZK within management's 2025 guidance—it was the firm's highest quarterly print in six years. In addition, it was just last month that the industry was rocked by news that a number of banks had suffered losses on loans made to financial borrowers. OZK wasn't one of them, but it plays into the idea that the credit cycle may be turning.

While this has been fuel for the bears, OZK's bulls would rightly point out that the bank has typically been a well-disciplined and conservative underwriter. It only writes senior debt for one, while OZK also requires developers to stump up a lot of their own money first—typically to cover land acquisition and a portion of the project costs—before it commits its own funds. An eagle-eyed approach to credit monitoring and extensive due diligence on each project also play a role.

As of the third quarter, OZK was still reporting healthy collateral cover, too, with its loan-to-cost and loan-to-value ratios clocking in at 50% and 46%, respectively, within its Real Estate Specialties Group. And between its annual pre-provision income ($1.1 billion), loan loss allowance ($530 million), and CET1 capital cushion (around $2 billion over OZK's regulatory minimum), the bank isn't exactly short of firepower to meet any rise in losses.

Away from asset quality, I also wonder whether the market isn't putting OZK in a kind of Catch-22 position. To address concerns about its loan concentration, OZK's management has been diversifying the bank's book away from RESG. Construction may still make up over 25% of loans, but that's down from over 33% a year ago after a surge in repayments. In contrast, the bank has been making a push to grow its Corporate and Institutional Banking ('CIB') unit. CIB now accounts for around 14% of OZK's total lending, up from just over 7% a year ago.

In a case of "damned if it does, damned if doesn't", the market may be concerned that these loans simply don't offer OZK the same risk-adjusted margins that RESG has in the past.

The above may well be the case, but lending isn't the only way OZK can make good money. Indeed, the CIB rollout opens up plenty of cross-selling opportunities for the bank, including on the other side of its balance sheet. While OZK has historically been a great lender, deposit gathering hasn't been nearly as impressive. Around half of its deposits were in market-like Time accounts last quarter, while just 12% were in more lucrative non-interest-bearing accounts. OZK's CIB clients will be a much better source of low-cost funds than its commercial real estate borrowers; so while it may end up sacrificing some of its historically high loan margin, it may be able to offset that by widening out its deposit margin.

A similar opportunity lies in non-interest income. This remains a paltry component of OZK's top line (8% so far this year), but it was up 11% year-on-year over the first three quarters.

Shares Remain Cheap

While the market has put OZK back in the penalty box, shares remain cheap. They trade for just $46.00 as I type, which is barely higher than the bank's tangible book value ($45.23 per share).

Per the last section, it's not like this low multiple reflects weak profitability. Given its leverage, that 1.9% average ROA has resulted in a circa 15% 10-year average return on tangible common equity. If one assumes that reflects OZK's through-the-cycle earnings power, then the stock would be trading for around 7x its normalized profit. This roughly chimes with the level of earnings that analysts expect the bank to post in the near term.

OZK's current book value multiple isn't a function of lackluster growth, either. Per above, the bank has historically grown its balance sheet at a ~13% annualized clip.

While OZK's multiples currently look depressed, this hasn't always been the case. Per the chart above, OZK's P/TBV ratio, P/E ratio, and its dividend yield are all at historically attractive levels. Granted, a re-rate may require a more favorable point in the credit cycle, but that presents an opportunity for the patient investor. On that basis, I remain bullish, and I am leaving my Strong Buy rating for OZK in place.

data.title: Ennis: Gross Margin Gains Point To Renewed Stability
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote on Ennis, Inc. (EBF) in September of this year, when we reiterated our 'Hold' rating in the prominent printing-related US outfit. Although reporting a normalized earnings beat in Q2 ($0.48 per share), shares have not managed to push on since the earnings release (Q2 earnings released on 9/22/2025), with EBF stock losing approximately 6% in the process. In fact, if we look at EBF's intermediate chart, we see that the shares are fighting to stay above multi-year support at just under the $17 level. Nevertheless, if this level fails over the near term, EBF stock has established support at approximately the $16 level, which is key.

The above point is crucial for EBF, as this company has a 35-year dividend-paying record, with the present annual payout amounting to a forward yield of close to 6%. Furthermore, one must not dismiss how shareholders have been rewarded in the past, concerning the generous one-off special payout of $2.50 per share, which was paid out late last year. Therefore, given that income-oriented long-term investors prefer lower prices in their investments (enabling compounding to take place at a faster clip), investors must focus on Ennis' total return potential as opposed to singularly looking at stock price appreciation over time. Suffice it to say, downside movement in the underlying is fine, as long as it is controlled, where the company's fundamentals have not become impaired.

Changing Business Landscape Doesn't Impede Gross Margin Gains In Q2

As the world becomes more digitalised, EBF continues to double down on acquisitions and niche markets in an effort to remain competitive. Proof of EBF's challenges can be readily seen in its financials with revenues, EBIT and operating cash-flow all well down over the past five years. Higher inventory levels, goodwill and declining cash also point to a company that is under pressure to report meaningful growth rates for its shareholders. The above trends are tied to recent spending-led acquisitions this year in the likes of NEC, PTI and inventory build-up due to the closure of the sole carbonless paper mill in the US. Furthermore, just recently, we became aware of the company's acquisition of CFC Print & Mall for an undisclosed fee. However, recent spending aside, there were some bright spots in the recent quarterly report with respect to margin levels and forward-looking cash-flow projections.

Although Q2 dropped slightly to $98.7 million, gross profit of $30.1 million resulted in a higher gross margin of 30.5% compared to the 30.1% in the same period of 12 months prior. A higher quarterly gross margin print hasn't happened for some time in EBF, so this trend is encouraging to say the least, especially in a time of significant change for the company. News on the $5.7 million judgment award to EBF was also welcome in Q2, more than anything, so full focus can now be restored to growing the company once more.

Cash-Flow Generation Expected To Increase

Moreover, our fears concerning the company's dwindling cash position also received a boost in the Q2 earnings report. Remember, EBF is a popular dividend-paying stock, which means cash flow generation and associated cash flow levels on the balance sheet are closely monitored by investors to ensure large buybacks and elevated dividend payments can continue. Inventory levels are expected to drop in the upcoming quarters, with cash rising as a result, as we learn below from CEO-related comments in his Q2 earnings report related-comments.

We expect cash flow to strengthen in the coming quarters. In the current and prior quarters, we strategically used cash to build additional inventory following the announced closure of the only domestic producer of carbonless paper. With this inventory now in place, we anticipate lower purchasing needs over the next few quarters, positioning us to restore and potentially enhance our cash levels. Year to date, we have repurchased 456,671 shares of our common stock for $8.5 million. Our profitability and financial position will allow us to operate and pursue acquisitions without debt, while also giving us timely access to credit if larger opportunities arise.

Valuation Remains Keen Despite Recent Growth Troubles

In previous commentary, we modelled using a popular dividend discount model that EBF is worth approximately $17 a share. This approximation is ideal for income plays (compounding occurs faster when reinvesting takes place at lower prices), especially when one sees that the company's trailing GAAP multiple comes in at a discounted 10.59.

Furthermore, when we take into account that $26.4 million in dividends was paid out by management from a GAAP profit tally of $42.2 million over the past 12 months, we can state that Ennis' trailing dividend payout ratio comes in at 63%. Therefore, when we as investors evaluate growth rates, we need to evaluate how much actual capital is needed to produce the company's earnings.

To the above point, EBF's return on incremental capital in relative terms remains elevated because it only uses 37% of its earnings to essentially grow the company. This can be readily seen in the projected rate of return EBF can give us as an investment, where we can use the formula below

Rate of Return = G + D/M

Where G = Yearly GAAP earnings growth rate, D equates to EBF's trailing GAAP dividend payout ratio, and M denotes Ennis' GAAP P/E multiple. Even if we only put in a modest 2% projected annual earnings growth rate, plugging our numbers into the formula gives us the following.

Rate of Return = 2% + 63/10.67 = 7.9%

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we are reiterating our 'Hold' rating in Ennis due to rangebound technicals, a keen valuation which incorporates a well-covered dividend and balance-sheet strength. Remember, due to large dividend payouts and buybacks, share-price appreciation is not the be-all and end-all here, but rather aggressive compounding of dividend proceeds over time. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Northern Multi-Manager Global Real Estate Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Commentary

The global listed real estate sector was a positive performer in the third quarter of 2025, as the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Index returned 4.07%. There was wide sector dispersion during the period with healthcare, retail and industrial REITs outpacing the broader REIT market. Residential, self-storage, and data center REITs were the laggards during the period. From a country perspective, Japan, Australia, and the United States outperformed during the third quarter. Germany and the United Kingdom lagged and posted a negative return.

The Multi-Manager Global Real Estate Fund (NMMGX) returned 2.15% during the quarter, underperforming its benchmark. Stock selection in the United States and an overweight to the United Kingdom were the main sources of underperformance during the period. From a sector perspective, an underweight to healthcare and out-of-benchmark positioning in towers REITs detracted. Sub-adviser Janus Henderson Investors slightly underperformed, primarily due to poor stock selection in the United States, specifically in hotels and healthcare. Sub-adviser MFS Investment Management underperformed the benchmark due to poor stock selection in the United States, and an overweight in the United Kingdom. An underweight to healthcare and non-benchmark positioning in towers REITs also detracted for MFS.

data.title: Calamos Hedged Equity Strategy Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy Overview

We position the Calamos Hedged Equity Strategy as an equity alternative, aiming to participate in market upside while limiting downside exposure. During the third quarter, the strategy delivered steadfast results, capturing a meaningful portion of the market’s gains. We believe this performance highlights the continued effectiveness of our asymmetric strategy, which provides upside participation while preserving the risk-mitigation characteristics that define our approach.

Market Environment

Although volatility remained subdued during the quarter, we are mindful of several potential catalysts that could drive market disruption in the months ahead:

  • AI Infrastructure Spending: Questions persist about the sustainability of massive capital expenditures in artificial intelligence infrastructure and whether returns will justify continued investment levels.
  • Trade Policy: Evolving tariff discussions and trade relationships create uncertainty for global supply chains and corporate earnings.
  • Monetary Policy: The Federal Reserve’s rate trajectory and its implications for economic growth and market valuations remain in focus.
  • Geopolitical Tensions: Ongoing international conflicts and domestic political considerations add layers of uncertainty to the market outlook.

We believe the strategy’s risk-managed framework positions it to navigate these potential challenges while maintaining meaningful equity market participation.

Performance Review

During the third quarter, the strategy earned 3.60% (gross of fees) and 3.47% (net of fees). We believe this underscores the strength of our asymmetric strategy in rising markets, as the portfolio advanced while preserving the risk-mitigation characteristics that served investors well during the first quarter’s volatility.

Performance Highlights

YTD Return: The strategy delivered a return of 7.62% (gross of fees).

With a low beta of 0.22 over the last three years, the strategy demonstrated lower volatility.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

Following a strong third quarter in 2025, the Calamos Hedged Equity Strategy enters the year’s final stretch with positive momentum. Our disciplined approach—anchored by a core long-equity portfolio and complemented by an actively managed options overlay—continues to offer participation in market upside with meaningful downside protection. This dynamic strategy differentiates the portfolio and positions it to navigate evolving and often unpredictable market conditions.

We build our investment process on a disciplined approach to risk management, implementing proven techniques to proactively mitigate potential risks while positioning the portfolio for attractive risk- adjusted returns. Our flexible, actively managed framework enables us to adapt quickly, seeking to capture significant growth potential while safeguarding against volatility.

In June 2025, we extended our hedge structure to June 2026 with adjusted strike levels, and it performed as intended throughout the third quarter. It maintains our target profile of ~65% upside participation and ~35% downside exposure over a one-year horizon. For risk-conscious investors seeking US equity exposure, this approach offers the possibility of attractive, risk-adjusted returns with built-in downside protection. We made no significant tactical changes during the quarter, and the structure remained effective.

While volatility compressed in Q3, we remain vigilant. Catalysts such as uncertainty around AI infrastructure, evolving trade policies, Fed rate decisions, and geopolitical tensions will likely drive future turbulence.

Outlook

As we enter the final quarter of 2025, we believe our hedge positioning and disciplined management approach position the strategy to continue delivering on its core objective—pursuing equity market participation with reduced downside exposure. Whether markets continue their steady advance or experience the volatility that subdued conditions often precede, we believe the portfolio remains well-positioned to serve investors seeking a balanced approach to equity investing.

For additional information, please visit the strategy’s profile page:

https://www.calamos.com/strategies/hedged-equity/

data.title: SSR Mining: Healthy And Still Undervalued In Gold's Repricing Era
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered SSR Mining (SSRM), I reiterated my Buy rating even though it was already up ~80% since first covering it in June and over 400% since the big accident in 2024, highlighting their strong cash flows, net cash position, and significant upside even without Çöpler mine contributions.

Right now, with a mixed report recently, some advancements regarding the potential reopening of their Turkish mine, and significant changes continuing in a direction that favors a higher price of gold going forward, I believe SSRM still represents a good gold stock to buy for gold mining exposure, with several catalysts that can unlock a lot of value going forward.

Internal Developments

SSRM’s Q3 FCF was -$2.4 million, which they say was "largely driven by inventory movements at Marigold and CC&V (Cripple Creek & Victor), as well as prepayments associated with development activities at Hod Maden," although we can see very solid operating cash flow of $57.2 million during the quarter. Still, during the first 9 months of 2025, SSRM’s free cash flow reached $135.28 million, a significant improvement compared to last year despite the big jump in CAPEX.

Excluding these working capital changes, SSRM reported an FCF of $72.49 million in Q3 and $268.5 million during the first 9 months of the year, although they now target production in the lower half of 410,000 to 480,000 gold equivalent ounces. This comes as the company produced ~102,700 GEOs during Q3, with CC&V proving to be their second-largest contributing mine, with exploration and development activities advancing at Hod Maden and their other brownfield growth projects.

Meanwhile, Çöpler remains uncertain even though they’re advancing, with the company’s CEO replying to a question regarding it during the Q3 Earnings Release, remaining fully committed to a restart, of course:

But we're closing off some of those technical aspects for the regulators to approve it. And that will be the key to getting the approval to start the operations. But as it happens, more recently, and particularly you've seen it in the press, so it's nothing that is not public.

Still, SSRM has recently announced the initial 12-year life-of-mine plan for CC&V, with this acquisition (bought it from Newmont (NEM) in 2024, closed in 2025) making SSR Mining one of the largest gold producers in the US. They expect this project to produce around $128 million in FCF over the first 3 years starting in 2026, with a 5-year average of $96 million (2026-2030) and an AISC of $2,135 per ounce during this half a decade. As a note, during the first 9 months of the year (despite the acquisition closing on February 28, 2025), the mine already generated $134.4 million in net income and $115 million in mine site free cash flow.

Financially, based on SSRM’s latest report, we continue to see a very strong position, with the $409.33 million in cash and cash equivalents covering their debt (which became current). They should have no issue repaying this, although refinancing could be more efficient since they have plenty of better things to do with the money.

Macro Developments

One development we’ve seen around the middle of November comes from Turkey, with new regulation giving the final say on mining permits to the president, which prompted criticism on several fronts. This seems to be a way for Turkey to circumvent previously refused projects by their state agencies and push the expansion of their mining sector, so it’s kind of a mixed development.

It’s still uncertain, but it can help create a more favorable environment for Çöpler’s restart or at least allow SSRM to sell it to a potential buyer that would be more interested in it now given the recent regulatory change, similar to how Alamos (AGI) sold its Turkish project recently. Note that the joint-venture that SSRM has in Turkey is together with a company that’s allegedly linked to the country’s president.

The macro environment continued to be optimal for gold, although I believe we’re looking at a delayed, inevitable reaction that finally happened as the market couldn’t ignore the need for a hedge anymore, combined with shifting fundamentals that will support higher gold prices throughout the cycle going forward.

This massive jump in gold is ultimately the result of all the things happening over the past half a decade (through most of which gold has been nearly flat), with global fear and uncertainty rising due to money printing, US rate cuts, the unpredictable situation around Venezuela due to Trump’s recent moves, AI overspending worries, US Government going through its longest shutdown in history, tariffs, and many other factors.

We’ve also seen Ukraine agreeing to Trump’s peace proposal with only some minor details to settle, which could be a big step towards the end of the war - although I don’t believe this will be that impactful for the price overall (with gold being up ~1% on the day the news came out) compared to these other factors.

Again, I believe this significant jump in such a short amount of time for such a "boring" commodity is the result of the past years seeing the market focusing on many other things (e.g. stay-at-home hype, war-related industries, AI, data centers, etc.) while the need for precious metal hedges built up while perhaps being ignored.

Once again, gold’s fundamentals continue to advance, while its potential remains nearly unlimited thanks to its sources of demand. Gold relies mostly on its demand coming from investing, jewelry, and central banks, which aren’t subject to the same market dynamics as its "normal" supply source, technology, which takes a tiny portion of the total demand. That source is subject to normal demand dynamics, where higher prices can result in lower demand/alternatives, while the bigger three don’t really follow the same rationale.

Meanwhile, if we think about other precious metals like silver or platinum, these are ultimately "capped" because a big chunk of their demand comes from real-life applications such as industrial uses, alloys, catalytic converters (platinum’s main use), electronics (silver), etc., while investing/banks/jewelry are much smaller parts of their total demand. This ultimately limits their upside, but I’d argue that it lowers the downside since there’s always a "floor" when it comes to demand, depending on the economic cycle.

Another important aspect about this fundamental change to keep in mind is that not even the previous jump in gold prices to over $2,000 was enough to lead to a meaningful jump in production. During this time (even recently), miners continued to go for consolidation through mergers and acquisitions, relatively few organic developments of current projects or even diversifying into metals like copper (see Barrick (B), Newmont, Harmony (HMY) or several others), barely replacing the depleted resources.

Plus, keep in mind that even if they wanted to, opening a gold mine from scratch can take even more than a decade in some cases (see B2Gold’s (BTG) Goose mine in Northern Canada), even post the exploration stage, with permitting and feasibility often taking years, followed by construction and eventually ramping up commercial production, all just to have about half a decade at peak followed by the maturity period, in many cases.

A relatively negative thing to keep in mind is that this massive jump in gold prices can also improve the feasibility of many already-known projects with previously failed feasibility studies (we can call them "dormant") because of production costs. But, again, these projects won’t become operational overnight, as even if the feasibility study is fine, we’re still taking about many years until the first gold pour. One thing that can lead to higher supply in the relatively near-term could come especially from the large-scale operations such as Chinese mines that can now keep producing or even ramp-up (potentially benefitting from state support too), making their operations even more competitive on a global scale.

And, there’s another key reason why I say that gold’s fundamentals continued to change drastically, especially since the pandemic, which is also a reason why I believe the current repricing makes sense despite its delay. As mentioned before, we can clearly see how the median AISC jumped significantly during this time, reaching over $1,500 per ounce recently (as of Q2; Q3 data not available yet), shifting gold’s fundamentals completely compared to even less than a decade ago.

Plus, to get back to the point I made before, keep in mind that even if the higher-AISC projects mentioned earlier were to become operational, that would also cause the median AISC to grow even more. This can then support these higher prices in the long-term throughout the cycle, while falling below $2,000 for example, would take out even more of the production compared to the current level, as you can see below.

I’ll give again the example of gold falling to $2,000 per ounce nowadays. This would lower today’s production by about ~15% in terms of sustainable AISC production based on the Q2 data available, while a slightly steeper drop to a bit over $1,500 would make about half of the gold produced today unsustainable.

Why this shows a massive fundamental shift for gold is because not even a decade ago, when gold fell to even less than $1,100 per ounce, that price was actually realistic given the market’s dynamics at the time. Back then, the median AISC was even below $900 per ounce (see the previous image), so at $1,100 per ounce, we still saw much more than half of the production running at a positive difference compared to the AISC.

In today’s market, gold dropping to a bit below $2,000 would probably have a similar effect on the production to what $1,100 had a decade ago, meaning that over half of the production would still be sustainable, while $1,100 today would make nearly 90% of the production unsustainable.

Still, I’ll point out that even though these are fundamental changes that will support higher prices throughout the cycle going forward, that doesn’t mean gold prices can’t fall. In fact, there’s definitely enough room for it to fall before becoming an issue for the supply, and no matter how bullish I am on the precious metal, near-term volatility can still affect SSR - both as a business and their stock.

Updated Valuation

Starting with the annualized level of FCF before working capital, which implies $358 in 2025 (using low $3,259 realized gold prices YTD and $36.72 per ounce for silver), I’ll go for a CAGR of 5% over the first 5 years now to take into account the CC&V cash flows, followed by 3.25% over the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll continue to go for a WACC of 9% (slightly above my estimated 8.84% for a better margin of safety (assumptions: ~9.2% cost of equity, ~4.2% cost of debt), with $817.42 million in non-controlling interest, with my adjusted extended net cash of $44.3 million (also including finance lease liabilities and related party debt) and 217.46 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of $6.24 billion, an equity value of $5.46 billion, and an intrinsic value of $25.12 per share.

Once again, keep in mind that this is without assuming any production from Çöpler and the realized gold prices assumed seem rational. As I said before, they’re literally sitting on a gold mine that could at any time be approved to produce once again, especially thanks to the recent change in Turkish regulation.

Conclusions

SSRM remains a buy for me, with strong financials and significant potential even without Çöpler, especially following their move to acquire Newmont’s mine. Risks remain, especially if the Turkish mine gets into big legal issues, but they can likely use it as collateral to offset this type of liability, at worst, while their other mines continue to be solid and their growth pipeline advances.

data.title: Eagle Point Credit Is At Lows, But We Still Like The Bonds
data.description:
CLO equity CEFs have been on sale lately. We find the entire business model to be terrible for the common stockholder of these vehicles, but they have often been invested in the fixed income of CLO CEFs. The recent selling pressure has called into question the credibility of these companies. We try to give them an improvised credit score one at a time. Today's case is about Eagle Point Credit Co LLC (ECC). We will try to put ourselves in Moody's shoes and assign a credit rating using the BDC methodology. Although it is not designed for CLO CEFs, it is the one that comes closest to our fund. Readers should take the credit rating with a grain of salt, as this is not a BDC, and the credit agencies do not have a methodology for rating these CEFs. We are simply seeing fairly correlated movements in the BDC and CEF CLO space and want to make a simplified comparison.

Company Basic Info

  • Тotal assets (Q3): around $1.54 billion.
  • Current market capitalisation: close to $833.8 million.
  • Nominal value of exchange-traded bonds: $277.5 million.
  • Nominal value of preferred shares: $113.9 million.
  • 10-year historical premium/discount:

  • Historical performance:

The 5-year total return is 15.84%, and the 10-year is 10.23%:

  • Leverage: close to 39.4%.

Non-leverage expense ratio: 5.84%.

  • Number of holdings: close to 306.

Below are shown the top 10 holdings of ECC:

  • Common stock: The common stock is currently trading at a price of $5.65, with around a 16% discount (based on last reported NAV), close to its lows. Below you can see the average discounts for the last 5 years:

Financial Instruments

  • Baby Bonds

ECC has four low-duration baby bonds: (ECCV), (ECCW), (ECCU), (ECCX). The call dates for all are past, except ECCU, which has a call date of 06/30/2027. All are with yield to worst above 7%, close to 8%. The highest are ECCW and ECCU. ECCW has a 5.95% spread to the 10-year treasury yield at its IPO date (03/17/2021), and currently, this spread is close to 4.58%. ECCU's spread to the treasury yield at its IPO (12/05/2024) is around 6.95%, and now it is 4.44%. ECCW's yield-to-worst is close to 8.20%, and ECCU's yield-to-worst is 8.06%. We are facing many questions about yield calculations, and we use the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is considered the most precise method for handling ex-dividend dates. It is a bit more work than just finding a random bond yield calculator, but at least we try to ensure the data is accurate. Below you can see ECCW's yields to maturity at different prices:

And here is the different yield to worst and prices of ECCU, as well as its yield-to-call curve:

  • Preferred stocks

Below are shown ECC's preferred stocks: (ECCC), (ECCF), and (ECC.PR.D):

ECCF is trading above par, while ECCC and ECC.PR.D are trading currently below par. ECCC's maturity date is on 06/30/2031, and its call date has passed. ECCF's maturity date is on 01/31/2029, and its call date is on 01/18/2026; ECC.PR.D's call date is on 11/29/2026. ECC.PR.D has the highest yield to worst of the three—close to 8.7%. Its spread to the 20-year treasury yield is around 4.71%, and currently it is close to 4.04%.

Below are shown the BDC OTC bonds that mature in 3-5 years:

The highest yield to maturity is around 7.8%, which is lower than ECC's Bonds.

Credit rating for ECC

Moody's considers six key factors in its BDC approach when evaluating the companies: Profitability, Asset Coverage Ratio Cushion, Asset Quality-Problem loans and Senior Secured loans, Cash flow and liquidity-Debt Structure, and Secured debt. You can see them below in the picture:

Profitability

To assess profitability, Moody's uses a 3-year average net income-to-average-assets-under-management ratio. ECC has approximately 5.6% for this metric, so it has to take the 4 score on the Moody's scale, which is equivalent to an Аа3 credit rating. This is the highest in the series so far, and it appears that ECC manages to be quite positive despite its terrible management fee structure.

Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio Cushion) (ACRC)

The higher the coverage ratio, the better the credit score. The leverage of ECC is around 39%. We calculated the asset coverage ratio cushion to be 24%. This is also one of the most important factors when assessing the company's overall credit score. The higher the ACRC, the better, as this simply means less leverage. The score we get here is 6, or an A2 equivalent. Keep in mind that we are using the regulatory limit for CEFs rather than BDCs, as the methodology states that other covenants may further reduce the asset coverage cushion.

Asset Quality

The company has no problem loans, but here we will assign the worst credit rating on the Moody's scale, since ECC invests mainly in CLO shares, which, as we know, is the riskiest tranche in a CLO securitization.

Cash flow and liquidity

ECC has no secured debt, giving it another top score on the scale, or the equivalent of Aaa. This seems to be the industry standard and is the main driver of the credit score. ECC generates high cash flows from its exposure to equity CLOs, which maintains liquidity even in higher volatility. The fact that the fund does not use collateralized debt reduces the risk of collateral calls and forced asset sales, which is a significant plus for liquidity stability. The phased maturities of the fund's bonds and preferred shares, with the earliest maturity not due until 2028, further limit short-term refinancing pressures. Below you can see how ECC's debt is distributed:

With a reasonably distributed maturity schedule and the income-generating nature of the fund's holdings, we are assigning it an investment-grade liquidity rating.

Overall Credit Score

The overall credit score of ECC is equivalent to Baa2. But we also adjust the credit score based on market capitalization, premium to NAV, historical yield spreads, and management fees. Taking all these, ECC gets a final score equivalent to a Baa3:

Conclusion

Our simplistic approach to ECC yielded a credit score similar to those of many of the BDC companies we analyzed using the same approach. The yields of ECC baby bonds are significantly higher than the BDC benchmark companies. It is up to the reader to decide how much additional risk the CLO equity entails compared to the secured loans BDC companies issue. The recent selling in CLO equity CEFs made us reconsider the credibility of the fixed income these companies have to offer. We definitely prefer to be invested in the fixed income when ECC trades at a premium, but even at the moment, we think that there is some Alpha in the baby bonds of the company.

data.title: PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The S&P Global Infrastructure Index advanced 3.5% in the third quarter. Benchmark performance was led higher by strong performance in the utility sector. Midstream energy sector and transportation infrastructure also advanced but lagged the overall index for the period.

Quarter

The PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund (PGJAX) advanced but modestly underperformed the S&P Global Infrastructure Index for the quarter.

In absolute terms, the Fund was led by strong performance in the utility sector (Sempra (SRE), NextEra Energy (NEE), Xcel Energy (XEL), Entergy Corp. (ETR), E.ON (OTCPK:EONGY)). Ferrovial (FER), the transportation company, was also a key contributor positively, as was Norfolk Southern Corp (NSC), a ground transportation firm. It was a difficult quarter for American Tower Corp (AMT), the communications company, and UK utilities Pennon Group (OTCPK:PEGRF) and National Grid (NGG) also had negative returns for the period.

Relative to the S&P Global Infrastructure Index, the Fund was hurt by stock selection in the utility (led by electric utilities) sector. Exposure to the communications sector, which is not represented in the Index, also hurt relative performance for the quarter. Stock selection within transportation infrastructure, along with an overweight to utilities and an underweight to transportation infrastructure added the most value during the period.

Key Contributors

Sempra

NextEra Energy

Ferrovial

Sempra operates as a leading energy infrastructure company in the U.S. and internationally through its California, Texas Utilities, and Infrastructure segments. Through Q3, the company took decisive steps to simplify its business, announcing a suite of strategic actions aimed at improving its risk profile and delivering value creation. Despite lingering earnings headwinds tied to higher financing costs and regulatory lags, Sempra reaffirmed its long-term earnings growth objectives and continued to make progress on non-core asset sales. Management’s consistent financial discipline, together with a focus on balance sheet improvement, have begun to restore credibility with investors. We view additional operational updates anticipated later in the year as potential catalysts for renewed momentum as the company pivots further toward its core utility strengths.

NextEra Energy remains the premier U.S. utility and a leader in renewable energy development. In Q3, the company delivered on earnings growth targets and maintained robust momentum in expanding its renewable project backlog. Investor sentiment stabilized alongside new regulatory clarity following the passage of the 2025 budget bill, which preserved full tax credits for safe-harbored wind, solar, and battery storage projects. NextEra’s strong liquidity and depth of pipeline activity position it well to navigate ongoing policy and financing uncertainties, ensuring multi-year visibility for its capital program. The company's focus on best-in-class execution and resilient demand for large-scale renewables continues to provide a foundation for long-term growth.

Ferrovial, the Spain-based infrastructure and construction company, continued to demonstrate operational strength into the third quarter. Core toll road assets, especially in North America, delivered consistent revenue gains underpinned by resilient traffic trends and improved dividend flows. The company’s ongoing simplification drive, focus on balance sheet strength, and U.S. listing all contributed to its positive equity momentum. Ferrovial’s positioning in long-duration, defensive infrastructure assets remains a key differentiator, and the outlook for continued strong cash generation and measured expansion into new markets supports further shareholder value creation as the year progresses.

Key Detractors

American Tower

National Grid

Pennon Group

American Tower remains the world’s largest independent owner, operator, and developer of wireless towers and U.S. data center facilities. During Q3, shares continued to underperform as investor sentiment was weighed by ongoing interest rate pressures and currency headwinds, particularly across non-U.S. assets. Additionally, the impact from its strategic exit of the Indian market persisted, leading to guidance dilution for the year. Despite near-term challenges, management has remained disciplined with capital allocation and continues to prioritize growth in developed communications infrastructure markets. While the long-term case for their global tower and data center footprint remains compelling, performance this quarter reflected both macro dynamics and regional portfolio shifts.

National Grid, a UK-based gas and electric multi-utility, experienced muted performance in Q3 as the stock continued to be affected by the interest rate environment and investor caution around its long-term capital expenditure plans. With UK and transatlantic operations focused on grid modernization, sentiment has remained cautious given sizable investment requirements mapped out through 2031 to enable green transition infrastructure. Nonetheless, underlying fundamentals and management’s steady approach remain supportive of longer-term value creation. We maintain our position based on attractive regulated utility exposure and ongoing dividend visibility.

Pennon Group, a leading UK water utility, underperformed during Q3 with shares pressured by regulatory uncertainty, inflationary headwinds, and market scrutiny over future pricing mechanisms. Ongoing debate around rate reviews and water sector investment cycles have kept valuations subdued, despite Pennon’s focus on operational efficiency and Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) credentials. While management continues to steer the business toward value-enhancing upgrades and stable returns, investor focus remains firmly on the regulatory landscape and clarity on future capex recovery.

Portfolio Outlook

Overall, listed infrastructure stocks continued their positive performance through September 2025, though investor sentiment became increasingly selective as concerns over policy uncertainties intensified. Trump Administration trade policies continued evolving, with domestic infrastructure benefiting from copper tariffs while global supply chains faced mixed impacts. The infrastructure sector demonstrated resilience amid broader market volatility, with defensive characteristics proving valuable during uncertain periods.

European infrastructure shares maintained their positive trajectory through September, supported by continued European Central Bank (ECB) monetary accommodation and sustained infrastructure investment programs. The European infrastructure sector continues benefiting from a valuation discount to global peers and supportive fiscal policies across major economies. European airports and transport infrastructure companies maintained their recovery momentum, while construction-focused infrastructure firms showed mixed performance as project execution and regional factors influenced individual outcomes. EU infrastructure spending commitments continue providing a supportive backdrop for the sector.

U.S. power generation and utility stocks delivered exceptional performance in September as AI infrastructure spending accelerated beyond earlier projections. Data center power demand reached unprecedented levels, with utilities securing premium-priced long-term contracts that provide exceptional revenue visibility. Nuclear power providers particularly benefited from their ability to provide reliable baseload capacity, addressing the 24/7 power requirements of hyperscale data center operations. The structural shift in electricity demand has elevated power infrastructure from defensive utility plays to growth-oriented investments, with electrical equipment manufacturers and grid infrastructure specialists experiencing exceptional demand conditions.

North American natural gas infrastructure experienced mixed performance through September as markets balanced growing power generation demand against policy uncertainties. Revised demand forecasts continue supporting midstream infrastructure development, with growing recognition that gas-fired generation remains essential for grid reliability amid accelerating data center expansion. Despite ongoing policy debates around fossil fuel development, practical grid requirements have elevated natural gas from bridge technology to essential baseload capacity, particularly given the immediate need for dispatchable power resources.

Electric utility and power generation sectors performed exceptionally well, capitalizing on unprecedented electricity demand growth from AI infrastructure expansion. Structural power demand shifts have created supply-constrained conditions that favor established operators with existing grid connections and generation capacity. Load growth projections now exceed historical planning assumptions across multiple utility service territories, with data center development creating multi-year visibility for capital deployment and rate base expansion.

Natural gas infrastructure maintains its critical role supporting new gas-fired power plants required to meet accelerating data center demand through the decade. Strategic pipeline networks serving growing power generation markets continue attracting investment, while companies focused on key transportation corridors benefit from structural demand shifts. The sector's focus remains on networks with strong operational execution and strategic positioning in high-growth power markets.

Transport infrastructure fundamentals showed resilient performance despite continued uncertainty from evolving trade policies. North American toll roads continued benefiting from solid traffic volumes and pricing power, while European transport infrastructure companies remained supported by fiscal spending commitments across major economies. Airport operators experienced divergent performance with recovery patterns varying by region and operational focus, though overall trends remain constructive for companies with strong market positions.

Communications infrastructure faced headwinds in September as tower operators contended with rising interest rates and foreign exchange impacts across international portfolios. Despite near-term pressures, the sector's defensive characteristics and inflation-adjusted revenue streams continue providing long-term value propositions. The ongoing 5G deployment cycle and densification requirements support underlying demand trends, though macro factors have influenced near-term valuation metrics across the sector.

data.title: Allspring Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

During the third quarter, municipal returns were positive (3.00%) and outperformed taxable fixed income asset classes, with the Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index returning 2.51% and Treasuries 1.51% and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returning 2.03%, corporates 2.60%, and U.S. high yield 2.54%. Within municipal credit tiers, A-rated bonds performed best (3.08%) and high yield bonds were the weakest (1.63%). The yield on the U.S. Treasury 10-year note ended September at 4.16%, down 8 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) over the quarter and down 42 bps from the start of 2025. U.S. equity markets were positive for the quarter.

With the downward movement of the municipal curve, shorter-end municipal-to-Treasury ratios decreased during the quarter while longer ratios remained cheap. Municipal 2-year, 5-year, and 10-year ratios of 64%, 62%, and 70%, respectively, were rich, while the 30-year ratio of 90% remains the cheapest portion of the tax-exempt market relative to taxable fixed income.

Net inflows were $11 billion for the quarter, and gross issuance was approximately $157 billion (a 3% quarter-over-quarter decrease), with tax-exempt issuance of $150 billion (a 0.5% quarter-over-quarter increase).

In September, the Federal Open Market Committee lowered its benchmark interest rate to a range of 4.00–4.25%. The municipal benchmark yield curve moved downward, with rates on the short end declining by as much as 36 bps and longer rates by as much as 30 bps. Market expectations favor at least one additional cut this year, though the size and timing of Federal Reserve action remains uncertain. The rate environment reinforces the case for municipal bonds as a stable, income-generating asset amid ongoing market volatility.

Portfolio Positioning - Portfolio Attribution* And Strategy

Duration—detractor

  • We were modestly short duration during the period, which was positive early on but was an overall detractor as the market rallied in August and September.
  • Municipal bond returns were positive for the quarter. We expect record issuance to continue through the end of the year and to outpace fund flows, which points to a weaker technical environment for municipals. Flows remained positive through the quarter, with year-to-date inflows at $29 billion, though total inflows remain below historical averages. We remain bullish on municipals because absolute rates represent a compelling entry point, and over the near term, we expect income to be the driver for the asset class. With the recent yield curve volatility, we expect to maintain duration closer to neutral and we see opportunities in the intermediate portion of the curve and expect to be selective about adding duration.

Yield curve—contributor

  • We were underweight bonds inside of 8 years, which was positive as shorter bonds underperformed. An underweight to bonds from 9–17 years detracted, as these maturities outperformed, but this was offset by overweights to bonds longer than 18 years, as longer-dated bonds outperformed.
  • The municipal benchmark curve shifted downward during the third quarter with rates falling by 19–36 bps across the curve, and the spread of 186 bps between 1-year and 30-year Municipal Market Data (MMD) tightened, down from 197 bps at the beginning of the quarter. We remain attentive to pockets of weakness or opportunity that may arise. With only 24 bps between 20-year and 30-year MMD, the continued compression makes extremely long-dated exposure less attractive for investors. In our view, the risk/reward trade-off strongly favors taking a more measured approach to curve allocations.

Quality—neutral

  • An underweight to AA-rated bonds and an overweight to A-rated bonds were positive, as AA-rated bonds underperformed and A-rated bonds outperformed. This was offset by an underweight to AAA-rated bonds, which outperformed, and an overweight to BBB-rated bonds, which underperformed.
  • Credit spreads remain tight. Despite the rate volatility, the spread between AAA and high yield credits remains near the tightest levels over the past two years, making credit relatively more expensive. Looking ahead, we believe credit fundamentals are well supported by the economy, though the possibility of credit spread widening remains ever present. There are opportunities for more risk-taking investors to explore the high yield space; however, as the July default by Brightline Florida demonstrates, security selection remains crucial in this segment of the market.

Sector—modest contributor

  • We continue to be underweight general obligation (GO) bonds and overweight revenue bonds. Controlling for duration, curve, and quality allocation, contributors to relative performance included an overweight allocation to the hospital and industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue sectors and an underweight allocation to the prerefunded, state GO, and electric sectors. Detractors included overweight allocations to the transportation and special tax sectors and an underweight to the local GO, housing, education, water and sewer, and leasing sectors.
  • In times of economic uncertainty, GO bonds backed by strong income and sales tax receipts along with essential services provided by municipalities are generally more defensive than discretionary sectors. We remain focused on these areas while also seeking opportunities in higher-beta revenue sectors where price dislocations create value. These dynamics underscore the need for an experienced team with institutional bandwidth to navigate an evolving market.

Issue selection—detractor

  • Controlling for duration, curve, quality, and sector allocation effects, security selection was negative for the quarter. Relative to the benchmark, the largest contribution effects were seen in the state GO, electric, and education sectors. Selection within the prerefunded, local GO, housing, hospital, transportation, water and sewer, leasing, and special tax sectors detracted.
  • The volatility observed in the first nine months of the year serves as a reminder to bond investors that municipals are credit products and not just a rates asset class. Security selection remains critical, as certain credits may diverge from the broader market due to forced selling, liquidity constraints, and structural factors. These inefficiencies can lead to atypical execution and present opportunities for strong risk-adjusted returns among liquidity providers. Prudent liquidity management has been a hallmark of our investment process, enabling us to capitalize on ongoing market dislocations while identifying compelling relative value opportunities.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is an unmanaged index composed of long-term tax-exempt bonds with a minimum credit rating of Baa. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index tracks the performance of U.S. municipal bonds that are not exempt from federal income tax. This index represents a diversified, investment-grade portion of the U.S. municipal bond market, covering issuers such as state and local governments and their agencies. These bonds are distinct from the more common tax-exempt municipal bonds because they are subject to taxation. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S.-dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid adjustable-rate mortgage pass-throughs), asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Definition Of Terms:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Average maturity: The average maturity represents the weighted average time to maturity of all the debt securities held in the portfolio. A relatively short average maturity results in smaller price fluctuations in response to changes in market rates of interest. A short average maturity subjects the owner of a debt portfolio to the risk that maturing debt will be replaced with debt carrying a lower interest rate. Average maturity is an important consideration for investors who hold bond and money market funds.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. Standard & Poor's and Fitch rate the creditworthiness of bonds from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Standard & Poor's rates the creditworthiness of short-term notes from SP-1 (highest) to SP-3 (lowest). Ratings from A to CCC may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the rating categories. Moody’s rates the creditworthiness of bonds from Aaa (highest) to CC (lowest). Ratings Aa to B may be modified by the addition of a number 1 (highest) to 3 (lowest) to show relative standing within the ratings categories. Moody’s rates the creditworthiness of short-term U.S. tax-exempt municipal securities from MIG-1/VMIG-1 (highest) to SG (lowest). Credit quality and credit-quality ratings are subject to change.

Duration: Duration is a measurement of the sensitivity of a bond’s price to changes in Treasury yields. A fund’s duration is the weighted average of duration of the bonds in the portfolio. Duration should be interpreted as the approximate change in a bond’s (or fund’s) price for a 100-basis-point change in Treasury yields. Duration is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Yield curve: The yield curve is a graphical representation of fixed-income security yields (usually U.S. Treasuries) at their respective maturities, starting with the shortest time to maturity and sequentially plotting in a line chart to the longest maturity. The yield curve is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Risks: All investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Bond values fluctuate in response to the financial condition of individual issuers, general market and economic conditions, and changes in interest rates. Changes in market conditions and government policies may lead to periods of heightened volatility in the bond market and reduced liquidity for certain bonds held by the fund. In general, when interest rates rise, bond values fall and investors may lose principal value. Interest rate changes and their impact on the fund and its share price can be sudden and unpredictable. Municipal securities risk includes the ability of the issuer to repay the obligation, the possibility of future tax and legislative changes and other factors, that may adversely impact the liquidity and value of the municipal securities in which the fund invests. A portion of the fund’s income may be subject to federal, state, and/or local income taxes or the alternative minimum tax. Any capital gains distributions may be taxable. Consult the fund’s prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled muni national long funds, the Municipal Bond Fund received 3 stars among 162 funds, 4 stars among 158 funds, and 4 stars among 115 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively.
